title: Gold for Iron
description: Gold for Iron is a pamphlet by Fellowship of Reconciliation. Prohibited by an Order in Council on 19 July 1918.
descriptionParagraphs: Gold for Iron is a pamphlet by Fellowship of Reconciliation. Prohibited by an Order in Council on 19 July 1918.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a pamphlet, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in New Zealand. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Prohibited by an Order in Council on 19 July 1918. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Gold for Iron is a pamphlet by Fellowship of Reconciliation. Prohibited by an Order in Council on 19 July 1918.
banningParagraphs: Gold for Iron entered censorship debates as a pamphlet associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1918 in New Zealand, where Attorney-General classified, prohibited, or restricted. Prohibited by an Order in Council on 19 July 1918. Prohibited by an Order in Council on 19 July 1918.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
